pattern: The cost to force two letters to match is fixed and symmetric -- the shorter hop between them going forward or backward around the alphabet -- so the classic interval recursion for longest palindromic subsequence just gains one extra dimension: how much budget is still unspent.
hints: Any two letters can always be forced to match; the only question is how many single-step swaps it costs, and that cost is simply the shorter distance between them going forward or backward around the alphabet (never more than 13).
This is the usual 'longest palindromic subsequence of a substring' recursion, except every state also remembers how much of the budget remains unspent.

For a range, either skip past one end (budget untouched) or pay to make the two ends match and add 2 to whatever the inner range achieves with the leftover budget; an empty range contributes 0 and a single character always contributes 1 regardless of budget.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, small budget forces a partial match
statement example, budget enough to turn the whole string into a palindrome
single character, already a palindrome regardless of budget
minimal budget, two letters exactly one step apart around the wrap
already a palindrome, spending the budget is never required
all identical letters, already a palindrome
budget one short of the cost needed to match the two ends
budget exactly equal to the cost needed to match the two ends
already a palindrome with an even length and a middle-repeated pair

stress.description: random lowercase string across the full length range with a random budget
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 200))
    s = ''.join(rng.choice('abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz') for _ in range(n))
    k = rng.randint(1, 200)
    return [s, k]
